content:
1	---
2	name: blueprint-writer
3	description: Update one blueprint chapter to reflect strategy changes, fill missing definitions or theorems, or align prose with the current Lean structure. Plan-agent-dispatched; one writer per chapter. May spawn reference-retriever mid-session when drafting reveals a missing source.
4	write_domain: "blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex"
5	read_only: false
6	can_spawn: true
7	default_enabled: true
8	dispatcher_notes: |
9	  - Dispatch one writer per chapter that the blueprint-reviewer's
10	    checklist flags as incomplete, lacking proof detail, or missing
11	    multi-route coverage.
12	  - Each directive must be precise: strategy context (the slice that
13	    matters for this chapter), required definitions/theorems (with
14	    enough mathematical detail to formalize), references, and
15	    explicit out-of-scope items. Writers do NOT speculate beyond
16	    what the directive lists.
17	  - **Authorize the retriever in the writer's --write-domain.** If
18	    the chapter might need fresh source material, dispatch the
19	    writer with TWO globs:
20	      --write-domain 'blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter>.tex'
21	      --write-domain 'references/**'
22	    The writer itself only edits its assigned chapter (its prompt
23	    body enforces that), but the second glob authorizes a child
24	    reference-retriever if the writer discovers it needs one. Omit
25	    `references/**` only when you are confident the writer will not
26	    need new sources (e.g. purely cleanup edits).
27	  - If the strategy has multiple viable routes and one route's chapters
28	    are missing, dispatch a writer per route to bring all routes to
29	    parity before any prover work begins. Do not push provers onto a
30	    route whose blueprint coverage is incomplete.
31	  - After a significant writer round, re-dispatch blueprint-reviewer
32	    in the same iteration to confirm the updated blueprint is now
33	    sufficient — do not assume the writer fixed everything.
34	  - If a writer's report contains entries under "Strategy-modifying
35	    findings", STOP and update STRATEGY.md before any further Lean
36	    work this iter. The writer is telling you the prose surfaced a
37	    strategy-level issue.
38	  - If a writer's report includes child reference-retriever dispatches,
39	    skim the new `references/<slug>.md` files before using the
40	    writer's output — the writer relied on them, so should you.
41	---
42	
43	# Blueprint Writer
44	
45	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.
46	
47	## Your Job
48	
49	The plan agent has decided that a specific blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) needs to change — to reflect a strategic decision, fill a missing definition or theorem the project needs, or align the informal prose with the current Lean structure. Your directive tells you which chapter, what must be there, and what counts as out-of-scope.
50	
51	You only edit the **one chapter named in your directive**. Your declared `--write-domain` should reflect that (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`). You do NOT edit other chapters, `.lean` files, or `content.tex`. Cross-chapter inconsistencies you spot go in the report's "Notes for Plan Agent" section — you flag them, you don't fix them.
52	
53	## Directive Format
54	
55	```markdown
56	# Blueprint Writer Directive
57	
58	## Slug
59	<slug>
60	
61	## Target chapter
62	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
63	
64	## Strategy context
65	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted for you — typically a paragraph or two describing what this chapter must support in the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself; the plan agent gives you only what is relevant.>
66	
67	## Required content
68	- Definition <name>: <informal description of what it must define, with the mathematical content the prover needs in order to formalize>
69	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
70	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>
71	- ...
72	
73	## Out of scope
74	<things that look related but the plan agent does NOT want you to touch this round>
75	
76	## References
77	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>
78	- ...
79	
80	## Expected outcome
81	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
82	```
83	
84	If a section is omitted from your directive, the plan agent decided you don't need it. Don't speculate beyond what was given.
85	
86	## Chapter format
87	
88	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
89	
90	```latex
91	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
92	  \label{thm:some_label}
93	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
94	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
95	  Informal statement of the theorem, using standard mathematical notation.
96	\end{theorem}
97	
98	\begin{proof}
99	  \uses{thm:another_result}
100	  Step-by-step informal proof. Reference blueprint labels with \uses{...}
101	  so the dependency graph stays accurate.
102	\end{proof}
103	```
104	
105	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
106	
107	## Rules
108	
109	### What you CAN do
110	- Add new declaration blocks (definitions, lemmas, theorems, propositions, corollaries) under direction.
111	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.
112	- Add `\uses{...}` cross-references.
113	- Adjust `\lean{...}` hints when the directive names a new Lean target.
114	
115	### What you MUST do
116	- **Keep the chapter valid LaTeX.** Don't leave dangling `\begin{...}` without matching `\end{...}`. Compile-checking is the plan agent's responsibility but you must not introduce syntax errors.
117	- **Stay within your chapter.** Your declared write-domain is one `*.tex` file. The Archon CLI rejects writes outside it.
118	- **Define non-standard macros in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex`** before using them — but: that file is outside your write-domain, so you DON'T touch it. If the directive requires a new macro, you note in your report "needs macro `\foo`" and leave the LaTeX using the new command name; the plan agent adds the macro before next iter's typeset.
119	- **Use mathematical, not Lean-syntactic prose.** Describe the proof in the language of mathematics — definitions, set inclusions, ring maps, universal properties — not in Lean tactic syntax. The prover formalizes your math.
120	- **Document every change** in your report.
121	
122	### What you MUST NOT do
123	- **Do NOT add `\leanok` or `\mathlibok` markers.** Those are managed by the `sync_leanok` phase + the review agent — never by you.
124	- **Do NOT edit other chapters.** Even when you spot a related issue, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" instead of fixing it.
125	- **Do NOT edit `content.tex`** (the top-level blueprint file that `\input`s the chapters).
126	- **Do NOT edit `.lean` files** or any other state file.
127	- **Do NOT write Lean syntax** — keep the chapter mathematical, not syntactic.
128	- **Do NOT expand scope.** Stick to what the directive listed under "Required content".
129	
130	## Reading references
131	
132	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
133	
134	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
135	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
136	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
137	
138	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
139	
140	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
141	
142	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
143	
144	Conditions for dispatch:
145	
146	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
147	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
148	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
149	
150	Dispatch (Bash, foreground, in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
151	
152	```
153	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
154	  --name reference-retriever \
155	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
156	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
157	  --write-domain 'references/**'
158	```
159	
160	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
161	
162	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
163	
164	## Workflow
165	
166	1. Read your directive completely.
167	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references.
168	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
169	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow.
170	5. **If a needed source is missing from `references/`, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and wait for it to return before drafting the affected sections.
171	6. Make the edits.
172	7. Verify the file is still valid LaTeX at a glance (no unmatched begin/end, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`).
173	8. Write your report.
174	
175	## Logging
176	
177	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/blueprint-writer-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path under `task_results/<parent-slug>/` when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
178	
179	```markdown
180	# Blueprint Writer Report
181	
182	## Slug
183	<slug>
184	
185	## Status
186	<COMPLETE | INCOMPLETE>
187	<If INCOMPLETE: which required items could not be written and why.>
188	
189	## Target chapter
190	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
191	
192	## Changes Made
193	- **Added definition** `\definition`/`\label{def:foo}`/`\lean{Foo.bar}` — <one line on what it captures>
194	- **Added theorem** `\theorem`/`\label{thm:foo}` — <one line on statement>
195	  - Proof sketch added: <Y/N + brief shape>
196	- **Revised** `<existing label>` — <one line on what changed>
197	- ...
198	
199	## Cross-references introduced
200	- `\uses{thm:bar}` added in proof of `\thm:foo` — verify `thm:bar` exists in <chapter or this same one>
201	- ...
202	
203	## Macros needed (if any)
204	- `\foo{...}` — used in <where>; needs definition in `macros/common.tex`. NOT added by me (out of write-domain).
205	
206	## Reference-retriever dispatches (if any)
207	- slug `<child-slug>`: requested `<source>`. Status: COMPLETE / NOT_FOUND / PARTIAL. Summary at `references/<slug>.md`.
208	- ...
209	
210	## Notes for Plan Agent
211	- <any inconsistency I noticed in sibling chapters that the directive didn't cover>
212	- <any structural concern: e.g. "the chapter is now 800 lines, consider splitting">
213	- <any reference I had to guess at because the directive's reference section was incomplete>
214	
215	## Strategy-modifying findings
216	<populate this section ONLY if writing the chapter surfaced a need to
217	change the project's strategy itself — not just the chapter content.
218	Examples:
219	- A definition you were asked to write turns out to require a property
220	  that the strategy assumes is automatic but is not.
221	- A theorem you sketched is provable as stated, but its consequence
222	  used elsewhere in STRATEGY.md does not follow from it.
223	- A reference cited as authoritative actually requires a different
224	  setup than the strategy assumes.
225	
226	When this section is non-empty, the plan agent must update STRATEGY.md
227	before any further Lean work this iteration. Do NOT silently adapt the
228	chapter to paper over a strategy-level issue — surface it here.>
229	```
230	
231	## Return Value
232	
233	Your final assistant message:
234	
235	- One line: `<slug>: COMPLETE | INCOMPLETE — <one-sentence outcome>`
236	- The path to your full report.
237	
238	Keep the inline return short. The plan agent reads the full report.
239

input.description: Find Galois descent and over-k commitment references in Jacobian.tex

content:
250:  The proof is split into three classical constructions plus the genus-$0$ sub-case absorbed into the same witness (the latter packaged as the explicit construction \cref{def:genusZeroWitness}, which routes through \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} directly over $k$ per the iter-127 over-k commitment). Each route produces the same intrinsic scheme $J$; only the universal morphism $\iota_P \colon C \to J$ varies with the marked point $P$. We outline each route and name the Mathlib infrastructure each one requires.
275:    sends $P$ to the identity of $\Pic^0_{C/k}$. It is universal among morphisms $f \colon C \to A$ to smooth proper geometrically irreducible $k$-group schemes $A$ with $f(P) = \eta_A$: this is the classical Albanese property of $\Pic^0_{C/k}$, proved by reducing to the case $k = \overline k$ (Galois descent), where it is a standard consequence of the autoduality of the Jacobian and the fact that line bundles on $C \times A$ trivialised along $\{P\} \times A$ correspond bijectively to morphisms $C \to A^{\vee} = A$ sending $P$ to $\eta_A$.
319:    \item[\textbf{(C.2)}] \emph{Rigidity for morphisms from $\mathbb P^1$ to an abelian variety.} We state the rigidity result over the algebraic closure $\bar k$, since the protected signature of Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} does not assume $C(k) \neq \emptyset$ and the genus-$0$ identification $C \cong \mathbb P^1$ is only available after passing to $\bar k$ (Brauer--Severi conics over $\mathbb Q$ are the standard counterexamples to a $k$-side identification without a rational point). The conclusion over $k$ --- needed only in the sub-case $C(k) \neq \emptyset$, where Sub-step~C.1 gives $C \cong \mathbb P^1_k$ already over $k$ --- then follows by Galois descent (Sub-step~C.2.f).
352:      \item[\textbf{(C.2.f)}] \emph{[DROPPED iter-127.] Galois descent to $k$ --- no longer needed.} Prior strategy iterations routed the conclusion of C.2.d through the algebraic closure $\bar k$ and then descended morphism equality back to $k$ via faithful flatness of $\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k$ (M2.c in STRATEGY.md, $\sim 4$--$8$ iter / $300$--$500$ LOC). The iter-127 over-k analogist (\texttt{analogies/cotangent-vanishing-pile-over-k.md}) verified that the three active pieces of the shared cotangent-vanishing pile (cotangent triviality via the functorial shear iso $(a, b) \mapsto (a, a \cdot b)$; \texttt{Differential.ContainConstants} as a $k$-agnostic Mathlib typeclass; characteristic-$p$ handling via the intrinsic absolute Frobenius $F_X$) all build directly over an arbitrary base field $k$ with no algebraic-closure or perfect-base hypothesis. The keystone of C.2.d is therefore established over $k$ directly, and \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} (the project's named rigidity declaration; see \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}) is invoked in the $C(k) \neq \emptyset$ branch of \cref{def:genusZeroWitness} with the supplied $k$-rational marked point $P \in C(k)$ feeding the pointing hypothesis. No base-change-and-descent step is performed. Galois descent of morphism equality is consequently \textbf{not} a Mathlib gap that the project needs to fill.
354:      \item[\textbf{(C.2.g)}] \emph{Mathlib gap statement (iter-127 over-k inventory).} The keystone fact of Sub-step~C.2.d --- that every morphism from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible genus-$0$ curve over a base field $k$ to a positive-dimensional smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme over $k$ is constant --- is \emph{not} present in current Mathlib (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) in any form usable here. The project records it as the named declaration \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar} (the filename and Lean identifier still carry the iter-126 over-$\bar k$ name, but the signature is already $k$-agnostic --- \texttt{[Field kbar]}, no \texttt{[IsAlgClosed kbar]} --- and the prose framing has shifted to the over-k reading; a low-priority rename \texttt{rigidity\_over\_kbar} $\to$ \texttt{rigidity\_over\_k} is scheduled for iter-128+). The keystone decomposes into the shared cotangent-vanishing pile of \cref{sec:RigidityKbar_shared_pile} pieces (i)--(iii): (i) cotangent triviality of a group scheme over an arbitrary base field $k$ via the functorial shear iso (no $\bar k$-rational-point lifting); (ii) scheme-level $\mathrm d f = 0 \Rightarrow$ $f$ factors through $\Spec k$ via the $k$-agnostic \texttt{Differential.ContainConstants} typeclass plus the iter-125-refactored \texttt{Scheme.Over.ext\_of\_eqOnOpen}; (iii) characteristic-$p$ handling via the intrinsic absolute Frobenius $F_X$ (no perfect-base / alg-closure required). Each is an upstream Mathlib gap-fill in its own right (see \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}). When $g = \dim A = 0$ the statement is vacuous and the declaration's conclusion is automatic. The reduction step C.2.b reuses the project's already-formalised Theorem~\ref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen} (after the iter-125 refactor, this is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Over.ext\_of\_eqOnOpen}, stated at the scheme level: source reduced and geometrically irreducible, target separated); the promotion step C.2.e uses standard reduced-source / separated-target lemmas, partially in Mathlib. The Galois-descent gap previously associated with C.2.f (M2.c and its companion M2.c.aux \texttt{geomIrred.exists\_kalg\_pt}) is \textbf{eliminated} under the iter-127 over-k commitment: rigidity is established directly over $k$, so no base-change-and-descent step appears anywhere in the M2 critical path. Total savings: $500$--$900$ LOC / $7$--$13$ iter relative to the prior over-$\bar k$-then-descend plan.
363:    \item Sub-step C.2 reduces, via the project's already-formalised rigidity lemma (Theorem~\ref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen} of Chapter~\ref{chap:Rigidity}), to the classical fact that proper rational curves on a positive-dimensional smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme over an arbitrary base field $k$ are constant (the keystone of Sub-step~C.2.d, packaged as \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar}). This classical fact is not in Mathlib in the form needed here; see Sub-step~C.2.g for the explicit gap statement and the over-k inventory of pieces (i)+(iii) of the shared cotangent-vanishing pile. Galois descent of morphism equality is \textbf{no longer} a Mathlib prerequisite under the iter-127 over-k commitment: Sub-step~C.2.f is DROPPED (see above).
372:    \item[\textbf{($\gamma$)}] \emph{The rigidity theorem for morphisms from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible genus-$0$ curve to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme, over an arbitrary base field $k$}: this is the prerequisite of the genus-$0$ sub-case under either Route A or Route B. The project packages it as \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} (\cref{chap:RigidityKbar}; the Lean identifier still carries the iter-126 over-$\bar k$ filename but its signature is already $k$-agnostic), gated on the shared cotangent-vanishing pile pieces (i)+(ii)+(iii) of \cref{sec:RigidityKbar_shared_pile}: cotangent triviality of a group scheme over $k$ via the functorial shear iso; scheme-level $\mathrm d f = 0 \Rightarrow$ $f$ factors through $\Spec k$ via the $k$-agnostic \texttt{Differential.ContainConstants} typeclass; characteristic-$p$ handling via the intrinsic absolute Frobenius $F_X$. Per the iter-127 over-k commitment (\texttt{analogies/cotangent-vanishing-pile-over-k.md}), each piece builds directly over $k$ with no algebraic-closure, perfect-base, or Galois-descent prerequisite. The genus-$0$ arm of the witness existence is packaged as \cref{def:genusZeroWitness} (\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZeroWitness}), which invokes \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} directly with the supplied $k$-rational marked point $P \in C(k)$ in the $C(k) \neq \emptyset$ branch and is vacuously true on the $C(k) = \emptyset$ branch (Brauer--Severi conics over $\mathbb Q$). Independently of ($\alpha$) and ($\beta$), establishing ($\gamma$) lets the genus-$0$ part of the witness be discharged unconditionally; the higher-genus part of the witness then remains under ($\alpha$) or ($\beta$).
406:  For the universal factorisation: given a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme $A$ over $k$ with identity element $\eta_A \colon \mathbf{1} \to A$ and a morphism $f \colon C \to A$ with $P \circ f = \eta_A$, we must exhibit a unique morphism $g \colon J \to A$ such that $f = \alpha \circ g$. \emph{Existence}: take $g := \eta_A \colon \mathbf{1} \to A$. Then $\alpha \circ g = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \circ \eta_A$, which equals $f$ precisely when $f$ is the constant morphism at $\eta_A$. This is the content of \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} (the project's named rigidity declaration of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}, established directly over the base field $k$ per the iter-127 over-k commitment): the supplied marked point $P \in C(k)$ feeds the pointing hypothesis, and the rigidity conclusion is exactly $f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \circ \eta_A$ as scheme morphisms. \emph{Uniqueness}: any two morphisms $\mathbf{1} \to A$ in $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)$ agreeing on $P \circ (-) = \eta_A$ are equal by the universal property of the terminal object $\mathbf{1}$ in $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)$ as a category (a morphism out of $\mathbf{1}$ in $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)$ is determined by where it sends the unique generator, here pinned to $\eta_A$).
418:  \item \textbf{Layer I --- direct definition.} The Jacobian of $C$ is defined uniformly as the underlying scheme of the Albanese witness provided by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}, carrying the four properties of Theorems~\ref{thm:Jacobian_grpObj}--\ref{thm:Jacobian_geomIrred} (each obtained by projection from the witness). The genus-$0$ case is included: the witness's universal property in that case carries the rigidity content for morphisms from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible genus-$0$ curve to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme. Per the iter-127 over-k commitment (\texttt{analogies/cotangent-vanishing-pile-over-k.md}), this rigidity is established directly over the base field $k$ via the named declaration \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar} and the shared cotangent-vanishing pile pieces (i)+(ii)+(iii); no base-change to $\bar k$ and no Galois descent of morphism equality enters. The genus-$0$ arm is packaged as the explicit Lean construction \cref{def:genusZeroWitness}: the underlying scheme is the terminal object $\Spec k$, the universal morphism in the $C(k) \neq \emptyset$ branch is the unique terminal map \texttt{toUnit}\,$C$, and the universal factorisation reduces to \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar}.

4:This chapter introduces the project's named declaration for the M2.a sub-step of the genus-$0$ Albanese-witness argument: rigidity of morphisms from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve of genus $0$ to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme, \textbf{over an arbitrary base field $k$} (no algebraic closure assumed; see \S "Iter-127 over-k commitment" below). The classical statement (Mumford, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Chapter~II~\S4) is
14:\paragraph{Iter-127 over-k commitment.} Prior strategy iterations routed through base-change to the algebraic closure $\bar k$ followed by Galois descent of morphism equality (M2.c in STRATEGY.md, $\sim 4$--$8$ iter / $300$--$500$ LOC). The iter-127 over-k analogist (\texttt{analogies/cotangent-vanishing-pile-over-k.md}) verified that all three active pieces of the shared cotangent-vanishing pile build directly over an arbitrary base field $k$: piece (i) cotangent triviality uses the intrinsic shear iso $(a, b) \mapsto (a, a \cdot b)$ (scheme map over any base); piece (ii) \texttt{Differential.ContainConstants} is $k$-agnostic in Mathlib; piece (iii) uses absolute Frobenius $F_X$ (intrinsic to $X$, no perfectness/alg-closure required). The Galois-descent step M2.c and its companion \texttt{geomIrred.exists\_kalg\_pt} (M2.c.aux) are DROPPED, saving 7--13 iter / 500--900 LOC. \textbf{The Lean declaration's signature is already $k$-agnostic} (\texttt{[Field kbar]}, no \texttt{[IsAlgClosed kbar]}); only docstrings, the variable name \texttt{kbar} (a legacy from the iter-126 over-$\bar k$ framing; rename to \texttt{k} is a low-priority cleanup refactor scheduled for iter-128+), and this chapter's prose framing shift to the over-k reading.
66:  \item[(i)] \emph{Group-scheme cotangent triviality.} The relative cotangent presheaf $\Omega_{G/k}$ of a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme $G$ over a base field $k$ (no algebraic-closure hypothesis, per the iter-127 over-k commitment) is free of rank $\dim G$. Per the iter-126 analogist's naming-idiom alignment (Mathlib snapshot \texttt{b80f227} has \emph{no} \texttt{AbelianVariety} file; the \texttt{GrpObj} namespace per Yang+Merten 2026 is the canonical home), target names are \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.omega\_free} and \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.omega\_rank\_eq\_dim}. A prover-ready three-step sub-lemma decomposition (i.a)\,$\to$\,(i.b)\,$\to$\,(i.c) for the iter-128+ build lane is given in \cref{subsec:RigidityKbar_piece_i_decomposition}. Estimated build: \textbf{800--1500 LOC} (iter-126 analogist's correction over an earlier 200--400 LOC framing; the triviality is the chain Lie-algebra-of-\texttt{GrpObj} + shear-iso globalisation + relative-cotangent-presheaf trivialisation on top of the project's existing \texttt{relativeDifferentialsPresheaf}, inheriting the presheaf-vs-sheaf bridge cost from \texttt{analogies/cotangent-presheaf-design.md}).
86:All three sub-steps are stated over an arbitrary base field $k$ per the iter-127 over-k commitment (see the chapter introduction, \S "Iter-127 over-k commitment"); the prose carefully avoids any reliance on $k$-rational or $\bar k$-rational point lifting, working functorially throughout. Sub-step (i.a) is the natural first target for the iter-128+ prover lane: it has the smallest signature surface (a finite-rank free $k$-module attached to the identity section of $G$), and no scheme-level globalisation.
221:  \paragraph{Iter-127 over-k risk register.} The globalisation \textbf{must} be formulated functorially via the shear scheme map $\sigma$ — which is a morphism in $\Over\,(\Spec k)$, valid for any base field — and \textbf{not} as a pointwise translation by closed (or $\bar k$-rational) points of $G$. The pointwise-translation argument requires either alg-closure of $k$ or a Nullstellensatz-style $\bar k$-point lifting (as in Mathlib's \texttt{pointEquivClosedPoint}, used in the alg-closed-base branch of \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Group.Smooth}); the functorial shear formulation sidesteps that obstacle entirely.
274:  \item \textbf{M2.b genus-0 witness} (iter-127 scaffold $\thm{def:genusZeroWitness}$, then later body iter-145+). The \texttt{isAlbaneseFor} field of \texttt{JacobianWitness C} for a genus-$0$ curve $C$ over $k$ branches on whether $C(k) \neq \emptyset$. When $C(k) \neq \emptyset$, \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} is invoked \textbf{directly over $k$} (post-iter-127 over-k commitment; no base-change-and-descent step); the marked point $P \in C(k)$ supplied by the universal quantifier feeds into the rigidity declaration's pointing hypothesis. When $C(k) = \emptyset$ (Brauer--Severi conics over $\mathbb Q$ are the standard counterexample), the field is \textbf{vacuously true} at the Lean type level: Lean's $\forall (P : \mathbf{1} \to C), \mathtt{IsAlbanese}\ldots$ is trivially satisfied when the type $\mathbf{1} \to C$ is empty.

input.old_string: \emph{Classical description.} The classical proof of the same statement proceeds in two parts. In positive genus ($g(C) > 0$), the existence and uniqueness of the factorisation come from the Pic-scheme representability theorem together with the Stein factorisation of the Abel--Jacobi morphism: see Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}, Route~A (sub-steps A.1--A.4) for the line-bundle / universal-property route via $\Pic^0_{C/k}$, and Route~B (sub-steps B.1--B.3) for the symmetric-power / Stein-factorisation route. In genus $0$, the same statement reduces to the rigidity theorem for $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A_{\bar k}$ over the algebraic closure (proved on $C_{\bar k} \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ after base change, using $H^0(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}, \Omega^1) = 0$ or Mumford~\S4's rigidity argument over the geometric fibre) together with Galois descent of morphism equality back to $k$: see Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}, sub-cases C.1--C.3, in particular the seven-step expansion C.2.a--C.2.g. The protected signature of Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} does \emph{not} assume $C(k) \neq \emptyset$, so the genus-$0$ argument cannot rely on the unconditional $C \cong \mathbb P^1_k$ identification (Brauer--Severi conics are counterexamples); the base-change-and-descent argument handles the no-$k$-rational-point case via vacuity of $\mathtt{isAlbaneseFor}$. None of this classical content is replayed in the Lean formalisation: it is bundled into the deferred existence hypothesis Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}, whose proof is the project's single explicit foundational gap.
input.new_string: \emph{Classical description.} The classical proof of the same statement proceeds in two parts. In positive genus ($g(C) > 0$), the existence and uniqueness of the factorisation come from the Pic-scheme representability theorem together with the Stein factorisation of the Abel--Jacobi morphism: see Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}, Route~A (sub-steps A.1--A.4) for the line-bundle / universal-property route via $\Pic^0_{C/k}$, and Route~B (sub-steps B.1--B.3) for the symmetric-power / Stein-factorisation route. In genus $0$, the same statement reduces to the rigidity theorem for $C \to A$ established \emph{directly over $k$} --- the named declaration \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}, whose signature is $k$-agnostic per the iter-127 over-k commitment (\texttt{analogies/cotangent-vanishing-pile-over-k.md}) and which is built out of the shared cotangent-vanishing pile pieces (i)+(ii)+(iii) over an arbitrary base field with no algebraic-closure, perfect-base, or Galois-descent hypothesis. See Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}, sub-cases C.1--C.3, in particular the seven-step expansion C.2.a--C.2.g, where sub-step C.2.f (Galois descent of morphism equality back to $k$) is \textbf{DROPPED} under the iter-127 commitment: no base-change-and-descent step appears anywhere in the M2 critical path. The protected signature of Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} does \emph{not} assume $C(k) \neq \emptyset$, so the genus-$0$ argument branches on whether a $k$-rational point exists. When $C(k) \neq \emptyset$, the supplied $P \in C(k)$ feeds the pointing hypothesis of \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} directly --- no $C \cong \mathbb P^1_k$ identification is required, sidestepping the Brauer--Severi obstruction (Brauer--Severi conics over $\mathbb Q$ are the standard counterexample to an unconditional $k$-side identification). When $C(k) = \emptyset$, the $\mathtt{isAlbaneseFor}$ field of the genus-$0$ witness (\cref{def:genusZeroWitness}) is vacuously true at the Lean type level: the universal quantifier $\forall\, (P \colon \mathbf{1} \to C)$ ranges over the empty type. None of this classical content is replayed in the Lean formalisation: it is bundled into the deferred existence hypothesis Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}, whose proof is the project's single explicit foundational gap.

In positive genus ($g(C) > 0$), the existence and uniqueness of the factorisation come from the Pic-scheme representability theorem together with the Stein factorisation of the Abel--Jacobi morphism: see Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}, Route~A (sub-steps A.1--A.4) for the line-bundle / universal-property route via $\Pic^0_{C/k}$, and Route~B (sub-steps B.1--B.3) for the symmetric-power / Stein-factorisation route. In genus $0$, the same statement reduces to the rigidity theorem for $C \to A$ established \emph{directly over $k$} --- the named declaration \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}, whose signature is $k$-agnostic per the iter-127 over-k commitment (\texttt{analogies/cotangent-vanishing-pile-over-k.md}) and which is built out of the shared cotangent-vanishing pile pieces (i)+(ii)+(iii) over an arbitrary base field with no algebraic-closure, perfect-base, or Galois-descent hypothesis. See Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}, sub-cases C.1--C.3, in particular the seven-step expansion C.2.a--C.2.g, where sub-step C.2.f (Galois descent of morphism equality back to $k$) is \textbf{DROPPED} under the iter-127 commitment: no base-change-and-descent step appears anywhere in the M2 critical path. The protected signature of Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} does \emph{not} assume $C(k) \neq \emptyset$, so the genus-$0$ argument branches on whether a $k$-rational point exists. When $C(k) \neq \emptyset$, the supplied $P \in C(k)$ feeds the pointing hypothesis of \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} directly --- no $C \cong \mathbb P^1_k$ identification is required, sidestepping the Brauer--Severi obstruction (Brauer--Severi conics over $\mathbb Q$ are the standard counterexample to an unconditional $k$-side identification). When $C(k) = \emptyset$, the $\mathtt{isAlbaneseFor}$ field of the genus-$0$ witness (\cref{def:genusZeroWitness}) is vacuously true at the Lean type level: the universal quantifier $\forall\, (P \colon \mathbf{1} \to C)$ ranges over the empty type. None of this classical content is replayed in the Lean formalisation: it is bundled into the deferred existence hypothesis Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}, whose proof is the project's single explicit foundational gap.
87:The three results above are formalised through the Albanese framework of Chapter~\ref{chap:Jacobian}. In the absence of Mathlib's Pic-scheme machinery, the Jacobian is defined (Layer~I) as the underlying scheme of an Albanese witness uniform in the marked point (Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}); the universal-property data $(\iota_P,\, \text{factorisation})$ is then carried by the Albanese predicate (Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese}) and projects directly into the three results of this chapter (Layer~II): the Abel--Jacobi map $\alpha_P := \iota_P$ of Definition~\ref{def:ofCurve}, its pointed property of Lemma~\ref{lem:comp_ofCurve}, and the universal factorisation of Theorem~\ref{thm:exists_unique_ofCurve_comp}. Existence of a uniform-over-$P$ Albanese object is the single remaining mathematical hypothesis pending the formalisation of either Pic-scheme representability (Route~A), symmetric-power quotients plus Stein factorisation (Route~B), or rigidity for $C \to A$ established \emph{directly over $k$} (genus-$0$ sub-case, via \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} per the iter-127 over-k commitment; no base-change to $\bar k$ and no Galois descent of morphism equality enter, and sub-step C.2.f of \S~C.2 is correspondingly DROPPED).
89:The classical proof via line bundles --- using the universal property of $\Pic^0_{C/k}$, dual abelian varieties, and (for the genus-$0$ sub-case) rigidity for $C \to A$ established directly over $k$ via \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} (per the iter-127 over-k commitment; no base-change to $\bar k$ and no Galois descent of morphism equality enter) --- gives the same conclusions but requires more Mathlib infrastructure than is presently available, as catalogued in the proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} and the milestone roadmap of \texttt{STRATEGY.md}. The Albanese-framework route used here is mathematically equivalent and bundles all of the missing infrastructure into that single deferred existence hypothesis.

# Blueprint Writer Report

## Slug
abeljacobi-galois-iter131

## Status
COMPLETE — three prose realignments applied in `blueprint/src/chapters/AbelJacobi.tex`. The "Classical description" half of the proof of `thm:exists_unique_ofCurve_comp` and the two paragraphs of the "Implementation route" section now describe the genus-$0$ sub-case as routing through `\cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar}` directly over $k$ (per the iter-127 over-k commitment), without invoking Galois descent.

## Target chapter
`blueprint/src/chapters/AbelJacobi.tex`

## Changes Made

- **Revised** proof of `thm:exists_unique_ofCurve_comp`, "Classical description" half (formerly line ~82). Replaced the over-$\bar k$ rigidity + Galois descent narrative with the over-$k$ route:
  - Genus-$0$ now reduces to `\cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar}` of `\cref{chap:RigidityKbar}` "established directly over $k$", flagged $k$-agnostic per the iter-127 over-k commitment (with explicit citation `analogies/cotangent-vanishing-pile-over-k.md`) and gated on shared cotangent-vanishing pile pieces (i)+(ii)+(iii) "over an arbitrary base field with no algebraic-closure, perfect-base, or Galois-descent hypothesis".
  - Added explicit `\textbf{DROPPED}` note for sub-step C.2.f (Galois descent of morphism equality back to $k$), with the rationale "no base-change-and-descent step appears anywhere in the M2 critical path".
  - Rewrote the $C(k) = \emptyset$ vs $C(k) \neq \emptyset$ branching to match the over-$k$ flow:
    - $C(k) \neq \emptyset$ branch: the supplied $P \in C(k)$ feeds the pointing hypothesis of `\cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar}` directly; no $C \cong \mathbb P^1_k$ identification is required; the Brauer–Severi obstruction is therefore sidestepped (Brauer–Severi conics over $\mathbb Q$ retained as the named counterexample).
    - $C(k) = \emptyset$ branch: `isAlbaneseFor` field of the genus-$0$ witness (`\cref{def:genusZeroWitness}`) is vacuously true at the Lean type level because the universal quantifier $\forall\,(P \colon \mathbf 1 \to C)$ ranges over the empty type.
  - Preserved the closing sentence "None of this classical content is replayed in the Lean formalisation: it is bundled into the deferred existence hypothesis Theorem~`\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}`...".

- **Revised** "Implementation route via the Albanese framework" first paragraph (formerly line ~87). Changed the trailing enumeration of route options from "rigidity for $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A_{\bar k}$ together with Galois descent (genus-$0$ sub-case, base-change-and-descent argument of \S~C.2)" to "rigidity for $C \to A$ established \emph{directly over $k$} (genus-$0$ sub-case, via `\cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar}` per the iter-127 over-k commitment; no base-change to $\bar k$ and no Galois descent of morphism equality enter, and sub-step C.2.f of \S~C.2 is correspondingly DROPPED)".

- **Revised** "Implementation route via the Albanese framework" second paragraph (formerly line ~89). Rewrote the parenthetical genus-$0$ classical-comparison clause from "rigidity for $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A_{\bar k}$ followed by Galois descent" to "rigidity for $C \to A$ established directly over $k$ via `\cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar}` (per the iter-127 over-k commitment; no base-change to $\bar k$ and no Galois descent of morphism equality enter)". The surrounding "classical proof via line bundles" framing was preserved verbatim (the line-bundle / $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ / dual-abelian-variety route remains the comparison being drawn against the Albanese-framework choice — that part of the section's narrative was not stale).

## Cross-references introduced

- `\cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar}` — added in three places (one in the "Classical description" prose, two in the "Implementation route" paragraphs). Target exists in `blueprint/src/chapters/RigidityKbar.tex` (named project declaration; signature already $k$-agnostic per its own iter-127 commitment, per the chapter's "Iter-127 over-k commitment" paragraph).
- `\cref{chap:RigidityKbar}` — added once in the "Classical description" prose to point readers to the chapter housing the rigidity declaration. Target exists (chapter label at `blueprint/src/chapters/RigidityKbar.tex` line 1).
- `\cref{def:genusZeroWitness}` — added once in the "Classical description" prose to anchor the $C(k) = \emptyset$ vacuity argument. Target exists in `blueprint/src/chapters/Jacobian.tex` (referenced from the M2.b row of the iter-127 RigidityKbar roadmap and from `Jacobian.tex` § C.1 / Layer I).
- No `\uses{...}` blocks were added/modified — the three edits are inside an existing `\begin{proof}` block (whose `\uses{...}` already covers the cross-chapter dependencies via `thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness`) and in non-block "Implementation route" prose (which does not carry a `\uses{...}` list of its own).

## Markers

- Did NOT add/remove `\leanok`, `\mathlibok`, or `\notready` (managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase and the review agent, per directive and project rules).
- Did NOT modify any `\lean{...}` hints. The three protected names (`AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.ofCurve`, `AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.comp_ofCurve`, `AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.exists_unique_ofCurve_comp`) remain unchanged.

## Macros needed (if any)

None. All commands used (`\cref`, `\texttt`, `\emph`, `\textbf`, `\mathbf`) are standard; `\cref` is already used throughout the project's blueprint (e.g. `Jacobian.tex`, `RigidityKbar.tex`).

## Reference-retriever dispatches (if any)

None. The three references the directive cited are all in-project files (already-formalised blueprint chapters and one project analogy file), no external source retrieval was needed.

## Validation

- Verified no remaining `$\mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A_{\bar k}$` formulations in `AbelJacobi.tex` (grep confirmed).
- Verified no remaining "over the algebraic closure" phrasing in `AbelJacobi.tex` (grep confirmed).
- Verified remaining "Galois descent" / "base-change-and-descent" occurrences in `AbelJacobi.tex` are all in the documented-as-DROPPED form (saying we do NOT use it / sub-step C.2.f is DROPPED), mirroring the canonical phrasing in `Jacobian.tex` § C.2.f.
- Verified `\begin{...}` / `\end{...}` count balance for theorem-like environments: 7 = 7.
- Verified the chapter-wide structure (`\chapter`, `\section`, `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\remark` blocks) was untouched outside the three edited paragraphs.

## Notes for Plan Agent

- The "Implementation route" section's enumeration of three viable routes (Route~A Pic-scheme / Route~B Sym^d-quotient / genus-$0$ rigidity) still leads off with Route~A and Route~B as live options. Both `Jacobian.tex` Layer~I and `RigidityKbar.tex` already treat the genus-$0$ over-$k$ rigidity route as the load-bearing route for the M2 critical path (and Pic-scheme / Sym^d-quotient as Mathlib-blocked deferrals). The `AbelJacobi.tex` prose now correctly removes the Galois-descent qualifier from the third route but does not re-prioritise the enumeration — i.e., the chapter still presents Route~A / Route~B / genus-$0$ rigidity as parallel options rather than calling out the genus-$0$-rigidity route as the canonical project route. If you'd prefer a strategic re-ordering or an explicit "Route~C is the project's chosen path" call-out, that would be a separate writer pass (out of scope for the present directive, which scoped to the three Galois-descent prose stalenesses only).
- Remark `rem:ofCurve_classical` and remark `rem:comp_ofCurve_classical` (lines 20–29 and 47–56) still describe the classical line-bundle / Pic-scheme description; they were not flagged by the iter-131 reviewer and they don't mention $\bar k$-rigidity or Galois descent, so they were not touched.

## Strategy-modifying findings

None. The three edits are pure prose realignments that bring `AbelJacobi.tex` into sync with the iter-127 over-k commitment already encoded in `Jacobian.tex` § C.2.f and the "Iter-127 over-k commitment" section of `RigidityKbar.tex`. No strategy-level issue surfaced during the rewrite.
